title: Tell the Year
developmental_frame: The last Communicate quarter meets a teen who can write for strangers, speak to rooms, operate in professional channels, and move institutions — and asks for the synthesis that adult thresholds demand: telling *their own story* under evaluation. At 15–16 this is developmentally loaded: identity is still under construction, so narrating yourself feels like fixing wet cement, and being interviewed feels like identity on trial. Which is exactly why the skills land so deeply now — the teen who learns to tell their story as *evidence arranged honestly* rather than self-mythology, and to sit in an interview as a professional exchange rather than a verdict, carries composure into every threshold that follows.
authentic_value_meaning: At this age, communication creates value when the teen can **carry their year through a keeper's door**: the story of the work told as an arc a stranger can follow and check, the interview survived as a conversation between professionals, the ask made cleanly, and — the closing act — every person who helped, thanked by name. The year happened. Whether it *counts* depends on whether it can be told. This quarter is where it learns to be told.

motivation_lever.mechanism: Every artifact this quarter meets its real evaluator: the year-story is published to the audiences who watched it happen and read by actual keepers; the interview practice culminates in real interviews the campaign's ask ladder produces; the ask meeting is the campaign's live summit. The audience is no longer practice — it's the door itself, listening. For a communicator built by this curriculum, that is the final and fullest version of the lever that started with reading one listener's face.

artifact: The year told, everywhere it counts: a published year-story narrating the arc of the work with receipts, an interview readiness kit (story bank, difficult questions, live drill records) proven in at least one real interview or formal conversation, the campaign's central ask made in person, and the gratitude circuit completed — every contributor to the year thanked specifically and personally.
success_criteria: A year-story is published where its audiences actually are, narrating the arc with checkable receipts rather than adjectives.
An interview kit exists — story bank, difficult-questions preparation — and was tested in live drills plus at least one real evaluative conversation.
The campaign's central ask was made in person, with the meeting run to full professional standard and followed through.
The gratitude circuit is complete: every significant contributor to the year received a specific, personal thank-you.

lessons.title: Find the arc in the evidence
lessons.concept: A year of receipts is not yet a story. A story has an arc: where you started, what you wanted, what resisted, what you did about it, what changed — in you and in the world. The craft is finding the *true* arc in your real evidence: not 'I did many things' (a list wearing a bow) and not the hero's-journey inflation keepers smell instantly, but the honest through-line your logs actually support. The test for every beat: could a skeptical stranger check it? Your year is unusually checkable — that's its superpower as a story. Arrange the receipts so they *tell*, and the telling becomes undeniable the same way the flagship did.
lessons.real_example: The baker's first draft was the list: certification campaign, recipe book, program application, revenue. Flat. The arc was hiding one layer down, in her logs: *a girl who started baking safe cakes for one brother discovered the town was full of families like hers — and spent a year building them what she'd wished existed*. Every beat checkable: the first cake (dated photo), the forty families (ledger), the canteen campaign (the principal's letter), the book (on its public shelf). Same facts, arranged as motion. The program's selection committee later told her which sentence got her shortlisted. It was the arc sentence. It usually is.
lessons.apply_task: Mine your year's governing documents and logs — bet memo, strategy, ledgers, build logs, campaign board — and draft your arc sentence: started / wanted / resisted / did / changed, in one or two lines a stranger could check. Then outline the year-story under it: four to six beats, each pinned to a receipt. Kill every beat that's decoration; the arc carries, or nothing does.
lessons.reflection_prompt: What arc did the evidence actually support versus the one you'd have liked to tell? Where does honest narration end and self-mythology begin — and how do the receipts keep you on the right side?
lessons.feedback_task: Read the arc sentence and beat outline to someone who watched your year closely and someone who didn't. The watcher checks truth ('is that really the through-line?'); the stranger checks transmission ('do you want beat three explained or cut?'). Revise for both.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help a friend find the arc in their own year — sport season, project, recovery, anything with receipts. Teach the excavation: list the facts first, then hunt the motion. Most people have a better story than they think and a worse one than they'd tell; the receipts arbitrate. That arbitration is the gift.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Arc-finding is pattern recognition over your own record, and the machine can propose through-lines from your (real) logs — worth one pass, because it sometimes sees a shape you're too close to see. Two locks: every proposed beat gets verified against the actual receipt before it enters the story (models embellish motion where the record shows drift), and the arc *sentence* must be written by you — it's the most identity-shaped sentence of the curriculum, and outsourcing it would falsify precisely the thing the story exists to prove: that you know what your year meant.
assets_needed: Arc worksheet (started / wanted / resisted / did / changed) + beat outline with receipts pinned
success_criteria: An arc sentence exists that a stranger could check, verified by a year-watcher.
A beat outline exists with every beat pinned to a real receipt.

lessons.title: Publish the year-story
lessons.concept: The year-story ships in two registers, same arc: the *public telling* — published where your audiences live, in your established voice, closing the loop for everyone who watched the build-logs and drumbeats (they were always owed the ending); and the *keeper's cut* — the tighter version that travels with your campaign materials, arc-forward, receipts adjacent, tuned to the door's spec (the hub's brief told you what to make visible; this is where it gets made visible). Both obey the recap discipline you've run for years: the hard parts told straight, the numbers real, the thanks begun. A year-story that reads true is the rarest genre on the internet. You've been in training for it since your first honest update.
lessons.real_example: Her public telling ran where her build-logs always had — the parent groups, the school channel — arc first, the brother's cake photo, the wobble weeks included ('exam season nearly ended this; here's what held'), the forty families thanked as a group with three thanked by name (permissions asked, of course). Responses: two hundred readers, a dozen family stories back, one bulk order, and — forwarded by the PTA rep — a note from the program's committee chair, who had *already read it* when her application arrived. The keeper's cut was one page in her campaign folder. But the public telling had walked through the door ahead of her. Published stories do that. It's why you publish them.
lessons.apply_task: Write and publish both registers: the public year-story (your channels, your voice, hard parts straight, receipts linked, permissions checked) and the keeper's cut (one page, arc-forward, evidence mapped to the door's spec, filed into the campaign materials). Log the public responses — they're receipts too, and the ask meeting may quote them.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Publishing the ending: what does it close that the year's individual updates couldn't? And which register was harder to write — the public one (vulnerability) or the keeper's one (compression)? What does that tell you about your next year of writing?
lessons.feedback_task: Ask two readers of the public telling — one who watched the year, one stranger — the transmission question: 'what do you now believe about this person, and what would you check?' If the believed thing matches your arc sentence and the checkable things check, both registers are working.
lessons.teach_back_task: Coach a friend through publishing their own year-story, both registers, receipts and all. The resistance you'll meet — 'nobody cares about my year' — is the belief your whole pillar exists to correct: audiences care exactly when the story is true, specific, and costs the teller something. Prove it to them with yours.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Both registers are claims-and-receipts documents in your established voice — the standing rules govern (your draft, machine tightening at most, every fact real, permissions for every name). The keeper's cut carries one extra weight: it will sit beside your application or proposal and be compared, possibly line by line, with your checkable record by professionals whose job is checking. The public telling carries another: the people who lived the year with you will read it. Between those two audiences — the skeptics and the witnesses — there is no room for a decorated sentence. Write like both are reading. Both are.
assets_needed: Published public year-story + keeper's cut filed in campaign materials + response log
success_criteria: The public year-story is live where the audiences are, with permissions handled and responses logged.
The keeper's cut exists, mapped to the door's spec, and is filed in the campaign materials.

lessons.title: Be interviewable
lessons.concept: Interviews flip your oldest craft: four years ago you learned to draw stories out of adults; now you're the one being drawn. The professional's preparation: the *story bank* — six to eight real episodes from your year (a hard decision, a failure metabolised, a conflict resolved, a deadline saved, a critique taken), each tellable in ninety seconds with its receipt, because interviews run on 'tell me about a time…' and the unprepared improvise vaguely while the prepared *select*; the *difficult questions* faced in advance — the gap in your record, the failure, the 'why should we take someone your age' (your Q&A steelmanning, pointed at yourself); and the *reversal* — your questions for them, because an interview is two professionals checking fit, and the teen who asks real questions announces which kind of candidate they are. Then drills, recorded, reviewed — the rehearsal discipline that built your first talk, building your composure now.
lessons.real_example: The video teen's story bank, indexed on one card: the client's-ending decision (humility + judgment), the eleven-day revision (critique metabolism), the handoff week (reliability), the marketplace rejection-and-resubmission (process under adversity), each with its receipt one link away. His feared question — 'you're sixteen; why would we give you real work?' — steelmanned and answered in drill until it became his favourite: 'because my record is checkable in a way most CVs aren't — here's a client, a public listing, and a professional who'll take your call.' His reversal questions — 'what does your best junior do that others don't? what would my first month actually look like?' — did, per the interviewer's later comment, 'more than the reel did.' Interviews reward the prepared exactly as rooms reward the rehearsed. Same law. You've known it for years.
lessons.apply_task: Build the kit: the story bank (6-8 episodes, ninety seconds each, receipts linked, indexed by the qualities the door's spec wants), the difficult-questions sheet (including the age question and your record's real gap, steelmanned then answered), and three reversal questions that are genuinely yours. Then drill: two recorded mock interviews — one with a friendly adult, one with the hardest interviewer you can recruit — reviewed against the tape (fillers, rambles, missed selections), fixed, and re-drilled.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which story-bank episode was hardest to compress honestly — and what did compressing it teach you about what actually mattered in it? And the identity question this quarter keeps circling: what's the difference between performing yourself and *presenting* yourself?
lessons.feedback_task: After the hard mock, ask your interviewer the professional's debrief: 'where did I sound prepared versus packaged — and what would you have probed next?' The prepared/packaged line is this lesson's entire calibration; adjust until you're reliably on the right side.
lessons.teach_back_task: Build a story bank with a friend facing any interview — university, job, team captaincy. Teach the selection principle: banks beat improvisation not because the stories are polished but because *choosing the right episode under pressure* is the skill, and banks make it possible. Then be their hard interviewer. You know the questions now.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The machine is a tireless mock interviewer — 'interview me hard for this role; probe my gaps' — and a sharp tape reviewer on your transcripts; use both freely in drills. The boundaries are the room's, permanent: live interviews are human weather (your Q&A training's oldest truth), the stories must be true episodes with receipts (an invented 'tell me about a time' collapses under one follow-up — and follow-ups are what interviews *are*), and composure cannot be generated, only earned through reps. Drill with everything. Walk in with nothing but yourself. That was always the design.
assets_needed: Interview kit (story bank indexed to spec / difficult questions steelmanned / reversal questions) + two drill tapes reviewed
success_criteria: The kit exists: indexed story bank with receipts, difficult questions answered, reversal questions ready.
Two recorded drills ran including one hard one, with tape-review fixes applied.

lessons.title: The ask meeting
lessons.concept: The campaign's summit: the door's central ask, made in person, with everything converged — the portfolio carrying the flagship, the keeper's cut carrying the arc, the interview kit carrying you. The meeting itself runs on craft you've owned for years (both-sides prep, the risk-led open where fitting, the mechanism close, the 24-hour summary), plus the summit's three specific disciplines: *make the ask itself unmistakable* — keepers cannot grant what was never quite requested, and a year of preparation deserves a clear sentence; *let the evidence do the arguing* — you built checkable so you wouldn't have to persuade, so point rather than plead; and *end with next steps whatever the weather* — yes gets logistics, maybe gets the mechanism, no gets the door-keeper question ('what would change the answer?') and the relationship kept warm. You have made this ask a hundred times at smaller doors. It's the same ask. It was always the same ask.
lessons.real_example: Three summit meetings, one season: the video teen's trial-shift ask at the production house ('one edit, real brief, your deadline — judge the work') landed the trial, which landed the role. The baker's program interview — where her reversal question about alumni mentoring visibly outran her application — landed the place. The guide-seller's third retained-client meeting landed a no with a door: 'not this term — but my colleague asked who made that guide.' Her follow-through note to the colleague opened client three by February. Three weathers, one constant: each teen left the room with the record intact, the relationship warm, and the next step named. That's what a summit meeting is *for*. The verdict is the door's business. The meeting is yours.
lessons.apply_task: Run the summit: secure the meeting through the campaign's ladder, prepare with the full kit (both-sides sheet, materials ready, ask sentence drafted and drilled), make the ask unmistakably, handle the weather professionally, close with the mechanism, and send the summary within 24 hours. Then execute the follow-through the weather demands — logistics, mechanism-rhythm patience, or the what-would-change question with the warmth maintained. Log everything; the campaign board's biggest entry is this one.
lessons.reflection_prompt: In the room: which prepared element carried you, and what arrived that no preparation covered? And the year-scale question: compare this ask to the first real ask of your curriculum life — what has actually changed, the asks or the asker?
lessons.feedback_task: Debrief the meeting with your decision-maker and your through-the-door informant: what landed, what would they adjust for the next room (there is always a next room). If the weather was maybe or no: workshop the follow-through with them — persistence design is a team sport, as your institutional quarter proved.
lessons.teach_back_task: Prepare a friend for their own summit ask — the unmistakable sentence, the evidence-points-you-don't-plead posture, the all-weathers close. Then do for them what this curriculum has done all year: be in the corridor. The corridor is where askers are made; you were made there too.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: Full rehearsal rights, as ever: drill the ask sentence, war-game the weathers, tighten the summary. And the summit's human lock, stated one final time because it is the pillar's foundation: the keeper is betting on a person — the one who wrote the checkable year, took the critique, kept the commitments, and now sits across the table asking clearly for what they want. Every hour of this curriculum built that person to be worth the bet. Make the ask. Let them see who's asking.
assets_needed: Summit kit (ask sentence / both-sides prep / materials) + 24-hour summary + follow-through log
success_criteria: The central ask was made in person, unmistakably, with the meeting run to full professional standard.
The summary went out within 24 hours and the weather-appropriate follow-through is executed and logged.

lessons.title: The gratitude circuit
lessons.concept: The pillar's final discipline is the one that makes all the others sustainable: the gratitude circuit — every person who materially helped your year, thanked *specifically* (the generic thank-you is a receipt nobody can cash; 'your advice about X changed Y' is currency), *personally* (the register each relationship deserves — a note, a visit, a copy of the book with their name in the acknowledgments), and *completely* (the list built from your ledgers and logs, because memory alone will miss the caretaker, the first customer, the cold-read stranger — and the missed ones are always the ones who'd have treasured it most). This isn't etiquette. It's the maintenance protocol for the asset your whole communication pillar has actually been building: a network of real people who have watched you keep your word for years and will open doors for you for decades. Stories end. Circuits keep conducting.
lessons.real_example: Her circuit, built from four years of ledgers, ran to thirty-one names: the brother (first customer, first cause — his copy of the recipe book inscribed 'for the boy who made this necessary'); the neighbour whose recommendation was her first channel; the caretaker whose cleanup plan taught her institutions; the practitioner, the programmer, the committee chair; the three customers whose exact words appeared in her testimonials, thanked for the words. Eleven visits, fourteen notes, six calls, one acknowledgments page. The responses did what completed circuits do — two came with opportunities attached, five with 'keep me posted', all with the thing that can't be generated, purchased, or campaigned for: people who were *glad to have been part of it*. Then she opened next year's bet memo, and began. The circuit isn't how the year ends. It's how the next one starts warm.
lessons.apply_task: Build the complete list from your ledgers, logs, and campaign board — aim past memory. For each name: the specific thing they gave, the register they deserve, the thank-you executed. Complete the circuit within a fortnight. Then the pillar's closing entry: reread your first-ever published piece and your year-story side by side, and write three lines on what the pillar built. File it where next year's arc will start.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Whose name on the list surprised you — the helper you'd nearly forgotten? What does the completed circuit reveal about how much of 'self-made' is actually 'well-helped and honest about it'? And which thank-you was hardest to write — why that one?
lessons.feedback_task: The circuit's own responses are the feedback: log what comes back — the warmth, the opportunities, the 'keep me posted's. That log is next year's approach-path map, pre-warmed. (Your campaign board's successor will thank you. So will you.)
lessons.teach_back_task: The pillar's last teach-back: put the gratitude circuit into one more life. Help a friend build their list from *their* real record — the coach, the first supporter, the stranger who helped — and execute their first three thank-yous. When they report back what came back, they'll have learned the pillar's deepest lesson without a single writing exercise: communication, at its root, is keeping faith with the people who received you. Everything else was technique.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: The final note of the pillar, and the machine's role is nearly nothing — the list-building can use your ledgers' automation, but each thank-you must be handmade for reasons that are, by now, the entire point: gratitude is the one message whose only content is *that a human meant it*. Four years of judgment notes have drawn one long line — machines for leverage, humans for meaning — and the gratitude circuit is the purest case the line will ever produce. Write them yourself. Every one. Then go start the next year, with thirty-one doors already warm.
assets_needed: Gratitude circuit list (name / specific gift / register / executed) + response log + pillar closing entry
success_criteria: The circuit is complete: every significant contributor thanked specifically and personally within a fortnight.
The response log exists and the pillar's closing entry is written and filed.
